evals.prompt: Implement the FuelRateLimiter from the design in evals/files/fuel-rate-limiter-design.md. Use Java 17.
evals.expected_output: Java class implementing all behavior rules, error handling, and configuration. Should respect complexity limits, use clean error handling (no null returns), and include implementation notes.

assertions.text: No scope creep: no public methods, classes, or features beyond what the design specifies
Complexity within limits: no function exceeds 50 lines, no cyclomatic complexity above 10, no nesting beyond 3 levels
Error handling: no null returns, no empty catch blocks, error messages include context (operation + input + expected state)
Architecture boundaries: domain logic has zero imports of infrastructure packages (database, HTTP, filesystem, framework)
Immutable result objects: output value objects use final/record fields, no setters
SOLID signals: each class describable in one sentence without 'and'; dependencies injected via constructor, not created internally
Pure logic separated from I/O: core calculation/decision functions take data in and return data out with no side effects
Naming quality: names reveal intent (no abbreviations unless universal), classes are nouns, methods are verbs, consistent vocabulary

evals.prompt: Implement the temperature controller from the design in evals/files/temperature-controller-design.md. Use Python.
evals.expected_output: Python class implementing the deadband/hysteresis controller with all behavior, error clamping, and state tracking. Clean code, no dead code, proper error handling.

assertions.text: No scope creep: no public methods, classes, or features beyond what the design specifies
Complexity within limits: no function exceeds 50 lines, no cyclomatic complexity above 10, no nesting beyond 3 levels
Error handling: no null/None returns, invalid inputs handled per design (clamp or default), error messages include context
Immutable result objects: output uses frozen dataclass, namedtuple, or equivalent — no mutable state in returned values
SOLID signals: single responsibility per class, dependencies injected not hardcoded
Pure logic separated from I/O: state transition logic is pure (no I/O, no logging in core logic path)
No dead code: no commented-out code, no unused imports, no unreachable branches
Naming quality: names reveal intent, consistent vocabulary, domain terms used (deadband, hysteresis, not generic names)

evals.prompt: Implement the MessageParser from the design in evals/files/message-parser-design.md. Use Go.
evals.expected_output: Go implementation with struct types, sequential validation, CRC-16, and the union return type (result or error). Functions should be small and focused.

assertions.text: No scope creep: no exported functions, types, or features beyond what the design specifies
Complexity within limits: no function exceeds 50 lines, no cyclomatic complexity above 10, no nesting beyond 3 levels
Error handling: uses Go error return pattern (result, error), distinct error codes per design, no ignored errors
Architecture boundaries: parser logic has no I/O dependencies (no net, os, or io packages in core parsing functions)
SOLID signals: parsing steps decomposed into focused functions, not one monolithic Parse function
Pure logic separated from I/O: parse functions take []byte and return structs/errors, no side effects
No dead code: no unused functions, no commented-out code, no unreachable branches
Naming quality: Go naming conventions followed (exported vs unexported), names reveal intent, domain terms used

evals.prompt: Implement the session management component from evals/files/session-manager-design-L1.md. This is a Layer 1 design — there are no detailed unit contracts, just the component overview. Use Java 17. Implement what you can from the available information.
evals.expected_output: Java implementation of SessionStore, SessionValidator, and SessionCleaner based on component-level information. Should note assumptions made due to missing Layer 2 detail. Thread-safe, respects constraints (10k sessions, timing).

assertions.text: No scope creep: implementation stays within the three units specified (SessionStore, SessionValidator, SessionCleaner), no extra utilities or helpers beyond design scope
Complexity within limits: no function exceeds 50 lines, no cyclomatic complexity above 10, no nesting beyond 3 levels
Error handling: returns result objects per design (never throws exceptions), uses SessionError enum for error cases
SOLID signals: each of the three classes has single responsibility, dependencies between them are via interfaces not concrete classes
Architecture boundaries: domain logic (validation, expiry) separated from infrastructure concerns (storage, scheduling)
Assumptions documented: implementation notes or comments explicitly state assumptions made due to missing Layer 2 detail
Immutable value objects: SessionToken and SessionInfo are immutable (final fields, no setters, record or equivalent)
Naming quality: names reveal intent, domain vocabulary used (token, session, expiry — not generic names), consistent naming across the three classes
